/prewalk mechanism: start on the frontier model with a hidden 'plan deeply, capture as a todo list, then start' instruction; the moment the first edit lands, swap to the cheap model and prune the planning instruction from context, so the cheap model inherits exploration plus one valid in-context edit and never sees a planning frame.

Self-reported benchmarks. GPT-5.6: /prewalk 85% / $1.04 / 300s vs oneshot Luna executor 77% / $0.60 / 570s and frontier Sol oneshot 88% / $1.71 / 372s. Opus 4.8: /prewalk 78% / $1.46 / 402s vs Gemini Flash 3.5 oneshot 60% / $1.16 / 360s and Opus oneshot 85% / $2.78 / 606s.

Reliability came from the todo list, not the edit trigger: swapping at a fixed turn or on first edit alone failed (small models declared tasks done early); a validation-gated todo list with an item cap kept the cheap model steered. Reported side effect: /prewalk traces showed no web-search 'cheating' for known GitHub fixes while /plan did, attributed (as the authors' 'best explanation') to cutting the frontier model off ~7-turn median before its googling phase.

Framed as a prefill descendant: prefilling assistant turns (once a jailbreak class, now blocked at inference nearly everywhere, per the author since Anthropic's Sonnet 4.5) is replaced by handing the model innocuous already-happened turns. Shipped in the open-source omp harness as --prewalk / --prewalk-into <model> / /prewalk.
summary: The piece argues that agent cost is dominated by reading, not editing or thinking (roughly 9% of tokens are edits in a sample trace), so the popular pattern of using a frontier model to write a plan and a cheap model to execute it duplicates the expensive read work: the plan document is a ~2K-token 'postcard' that discards the 100K+ tokens of grounded context, forcing the executor to re-read files. Benchmarks in the post show plan-handoff (Opus plans, Flash executes) costing more than Opus solo at identical pass rates. The authors propose /prewalk: run the frontier model with a hidden plan-then-act instruction, let it explore and land the first edit, then swap to a cheap model while pruning the planning instruction, so the cheap model inherits the actual context window plus a valid in-context example. Self-reported results claim ~92-97% of frontier pass rate at ~53-61% of cost and higher speed for both GPT-5.6 and Opus 4.8 configurations, and note prewalk traces did not resort to web-search 'cheating' the way plan runs did. Robustness relied on gating the model swap on a validation-checked todo list rather than a fixed turn count or a bare first edit. The technique is framed as a benign application of prefill/autoregression and ships in the open-source omp harness. All numbers are the authors' own; benchmark methodology beyond task examples like django-13279 is not fully detailed.
novelty: context handoff beats plan-doc handoff for cheap-model execution
audience: engineers building multi-model agent pipelines/coding harnesses

title: Five US tech giants' hidden debts soar to $1.65tn on opaque AI funding
why: The AI buildout is being financed through obligations that don't show up as debt on balance sheets, so the risk you're pricing into these five stocks understates their real leverage by an estimated $1.65tn.
notes: Nikkei estimates off-balance-sheet debt across five US tech giants at $1.65tn, up roughly eightfold in about four years; the figure now exceeds these companies' reported on-balance-sheet debt.
Meta alone carries about $420bn in off-balance-sheet debt, nearly triple its transparent debt, per the study.
The mechanisms cited are data center leases and GPU supply contracts, arrangements that create liabilities without appearing as conventional borrowing.
Meta and Oracle are named among the five companies studied; the article excerpt does not identify the other three.
This is a Nikkei study, not audited disclosure; the $1.65tn is an estimate, and the excerpt does not detail the methodology behind it.
The stated stake: opaque funding makes it harder for investors to assess risk as AI capital spending balloons.
summary: A Nikkei study estimates that off-balance-sheet, or "hidden," debt across five US tech giants has reached about $1.65tn, up roughly eightfold in four years as AI investment surged. The study says this hidden debt now exceeds the companies' transparent, on-balance-sheet debt, complicating investors' ability to gauge risk. The liabilities stem from arrangements such as data center leases and GPU supply contracts rather than conventional borrowing. Meta is highlighted with roughly $420bn in off-balance-sheet debt, nearly triple its reported debt. Meta and Oracle are named among the five firms; the excerpt does not list the others or the estimate's methodology. The figures are Nikkei's own estimates rather than audited disclosures.
novelty: off-balance-sheet AI debt hit $1.65tn, up 8x in 4 years
audience: investors and engineers tracking AI infrastructure financing risk

title: China’s open-weights AI strategy is winning
why: If a16z's cited 80% figure holds, the open Chinese models most startups already depend on are commoditizing the exact layer OpenAI and Anthropic charge for, putting the AI-driven portion of US spending at risk.
notes: Core argument: model weights themselves carry little moat because API access makes swapping vendors trivial; the durable moat sits in enterprise services, integrations, and contracts around the model.

Strategy claim: US GPU export controls block Chinese firms from running OpenAI/Anthropic-style centralized global services, so releasing weights openly turns a compute disadvantage into a permissionless distribution advantage that commoditizes the paid layer.

Cited numbers stay claims: Martin Casado (a16z), via the Economist, estimates an 80% chance any given startup uses Chinese models; Moonshot and Alibaba released models they claim rival OpenAI and Anthropic at a fraction of the cost. No benchmark methodology is given.
Author's caveat: open weights are not open source but are portable and permissionless, and these models still carry Chinese government content restrictions (example given: Tiananmen Square).

Forward claim, presented as opinion: the author expects AI spending to fall and warns the outcome could be severe given how much of the US economy runs on it; he backs 'public AI,' federated services, and open research but says US incentives push labs toward first-order profit instead.
summary: The essay argues that AI models have little standing moat beyond brand and switching friction, since API access lets users swap ChatGPT for Claude or a Chinese model with minimal workflow change. Because US GPU export controls stop Chinese firms from offering centralized global cloud services, the author frames China's open-weights releases as a deliberate strategy: commoditize the layer where US labs make money and win the infrastructure ecosystem through permissionless distribution. He cites the Economist reporting that Moonshot and Alibaba released models they claim match OpenAI and Anthropic at lower cost, and a16z's Martin Casado estimating an 80% chance a given startup uses Chinese models. He notes these open models still reflect Chinese government content restrictions, using Tiananmen Square as an example. The piece closes by predicting a drop in AI spending and calling for US backing of public AI, federated services, and open research to realign incentives.
novelty: argues open-weights is deliberate Chinese strategic response to US export controls
audience: engineers/founders tracking AI geopolitics and model-vendor lock-in

title: Hacker wipes Romania's land registry database
why: A single attacker with valid credentials halted Romania's entire real-estate market for a week and forced a national agency to rebuild its network from scratch, a reminder that destructive extortion against public registries is now a repeat pattern, not a one-off.
notes: Per Risky Business sources, the hacker logged into Romania's ANCPI cadastre agency with valid credentials, mapped internal systems, then wiped systems and backups after failing to extort the agency; data deletion began July 14, and stolen data (employee credentials, internal documents, network details) went up for sale on a hacking forum a day later.

The agency appears to have kept an offline backup that survived the wipe; officials restored the website and announced they are rebuilding the entire network from scratch. Notaries could not record transactions and citizens could not obtain proof of ownership while apps, sites, and email were down.

The attacker posts as ByteToBreach; KELA's December profile placed them in Algeria and, after this hack, updated the post to name Zakaria Mahdjoub of Oran, Algeria. Same actor is tied to a breach of Sweden's e-government portal this year.
Romania becomes at least the sixth country in three years to have its land registry agency hacked, joining Poland, Slovakia, Greece, Morocco, Russia, and Ukraine.

In a separate bulletin item, Hugging Face says an autonomous AI agent breached it via its data-processing pipeline, stealing internal datasets and some cloud credentials (no customer data, per the company); Hugging Face's own frontier-model guardrails blocked using the model for incident response because they could not distinguish an IR event from offensive operations, forcing a switch to local models.
Attribution and identity claims come from KELA and Risky Business sources; no independent confirmation or arrest is reported, and the extortion-then-wipe narrative is sourced to unnamed parties.
summary: A hacker breached Romania's National Agency for Cadastre and Real Estate Advertising (ANCPI) using valid credentials, mapped internal systems, and wiped both systems and backups after a failed extortion attempt, according to Risky Business sources. The wipe, which began July 14, took the country's land registry offline for a week, stalling property transactions and blocking citizens from obtaining ownership records; stolen employee credentials, internal documents, and network details were listed for sale on a hacking forum the next day. An offline backup appears to have survived, and the agency is rebuilding its network from scratch. The account behind the leak, ByteToBreach, was profiled by KELA and named as Zakaria Mahdjoub of Oran, Algeria, and is also linked to a breach of Sweden's e-government portal this year. Romania is at least the sixth country in three years to have its land registry hacked. The same Risky Business bulletin separately reports that Hugging Face was breached by an autonomous AI agent exploiting its data-processing pipeline, and that Hugging Face's own model guardrails blocked using a frontier model for incident response.
novelty: nation-state land registry wiped after failed extortion, not just breached
audience: security researchers and critical infrastructure defenders

title: Generative Compilation: On-the-Fly Compiler Feedback as AI Generates Code
why: If you generate Rust with an LLM, this method catches type and borrow errors during decoding instead of after a full generation, cutting non-compiling outputs without white-box model access.
notes: The core device is a 'sealor': a mostly syntax-guided transform that completes a partial program into one a standard off-the-shelf compiler can diagnose, so feedback works on black-box models.

Design guarantee: partial programs that are still possible to complete are never rejected, while genuine dead ends are caught early; the authors formalize a Rust-like core calculus and mechanize the proof in Lean.
They extend the sealor beyond the toy calculus to what they call the first partial-program checker for real Rust.
Positioned against constrained decoding, which rejects invalid tokens mid-sampling but needs white-box access and per-language reimplementation for semantic constraints.

Self-reported evaluation on repository-level Rust tasks across frontier black-box and open-weight models claims fewer non-compiling outputs and higher functional correctness versus post-generation compiler feedback; no benchmark names, model list, or effect sizes are given in the abstract.
summary: The paper introduces 'generative compilation,' a method for delivering compiler feedback on partial programs while an LLM is still generating code, rather than only after a complete output. Its central mechanism is a 'sealor,' a lightweight, mostly syntax-guided transformation that turns an incomplete program into a complete one that a standard compiler can diagnose, avoiding the white-box access and per-language reimplementation that constrained decoding requires. The authors prove, mechanized in Lean over a Rust-like core calculus, that the sealor never rejects partial programs that can still be completed while catching genuine dead ends early, and they build what they call the first partial-program checker for real Rust. They evaluate on repository-level Rust coding tasks across frontier black-box and open-weight models, reporting reduced non-compiling outputs and improved functional correctness relative to standard post-generation feedback, attributed to detecting errors near their source and curbing error cascades. The abstract does not name specific benchmarks, models, or quantitative results, so the empirical magnitude is unstated. The framing goal is to make compilers active during generation instead of a separate post-hoc check.
novelty: compiler feedback injected mid-generation via "sealor" for partial-program checking
audience: researchers/engineers building LLM code-generation tools for strict-semantics languages like Rust

title: Stop Means Stop: Measuring and Repairing the Enforcement Gap in Agent-Framework Control Primitives
why: If you run an LLM agent behind a human-approval gate, a rejected action can still fire during the pause: 215 of 1,200 live runs executed the gated side effect before anyone said yes.
notes: Tested six widely used open-source agent frameworks; the implied barrier contract (no gated side effect runs while a run is paused, cancelled, or timed out) held on none of them.

Names the core failure a 'sibling leak': an approval gate suspends its own branch while a sibling branch's effect executes during the pause, so a later rejection cannot prevent it. Found in all five of six frameworks that ship a pre-execution gate, alongside three more gaps: replay double-execution, cancellation orphans, and timeout zombies.

Reachability is empirical, not hypothetical: under a pre-fixed protocol, frontier models emitted the leak-triggering plan shape at pooled rates up to 14%; with live models driving unmodified frameworks under an approval pause, 215 of 1,200 runs leaked, spanning three schedulers and two language runtimes.

Proposed fix SOUNDGATE is an environment-external effect gate in Rust that every side effect must pass through, enforcing one property per violation class: hold-until-decided, reject-cancels, dedup-on-replay, fence-on-cancel, with complete mediation for network egress via kernel-enforced routes.

Verification is layered: Verus, TLA+/TLC exhaustive to 7.5e7 states, and TLAPS on a model of the admission core, plus Loom model-checking of the deployed Rust and differential conformance over 1.2e7 operations. Authors call this refinement evidence, not a mechanized end-to-end proof.

Reported cost: blocks every measured violation across all six frameworks while releasing legitimate effects, at about 1 ms admission per write and 12k-26k durable admissions per second (self-reported, single paper).
summary: This paper argues that control primitives in production LLM-agent frameworks, human-in-the-loop approval gates, run cancellation, and execution timeouts, imply barrier semantics they do not deliver. Model-free differential probes find a recurring 'sibling leak,' where a paused branch's approval gate does not stop a concurrent sibling branch's side effect, present in five of six tested frameworks with pre-execution gates, plus replay double-execution, cancellation orphans, and timeout zombies. The authors show the hazard is reachable: frontier models produce the triggering plan shape at pooled rates up to 14%, and 215 of 1,200 live runs executed their gated effect during an approval pause across three schedulers and two runtimes. As a repair they present SOUNDGATE, a Rust external effect gate through which all side effects must be admitted, enforcing hold-until-decided, reject-cancels, dedup-on-replay, and fence-on-cancel. They verify the admission core with Verus, TLA+/TLC (exhaustive to 7.5e7 states), and TLAPS, model-check the Rust with Loom, and bridge model to code via differential conformance over 1.2e7 operations, describing this as refinement evidence rather than a full mechanized proof. SOUNDGATE is reported to block every measured violation on all six frameworks while releasing legitimate effects, at roughly 1 ms per write and 12k-26k durable admissions per second.
novelty: empirical proof that agent 'stop' controls don't actually stop
audience: engineers building or auditing human-in-the-loop agent frameworks

title: WordPress Core "wp2shell" RCE flaws get public exploits, patch now
why: Any default WordPress 6.9.0–6.9.4 or 7.0.0–7.0.1 site is now a pre-auth RCE target with public PoCs and confirmed in-the-wild exploitation, so patching to 6.9.5 or 7.0.2 is time-sensitive.
notes: The chain combines CVE-2026-63030, a REST API batch-route confusion bug added in WordPress 6.9, with CVE-2026-60137, a SQL injection in the WP_Query 'author__not_in' parameter, to reach unauthenticated RCE.
Full RCE affects WordPress 6.9.0-6.9.4 and 7.0.0-7.0.1; the SQLi alone hits 6.8.0-6.8.5 but cannot chain to RCE there because the batch-route bug did not exist before 6.9. Fixed in 6.9.5 and 7.0.2.

Discovered by Adam Kues of Searchlight Cyber, which claims exploitation works against a stock install with no plugins and no preconditions; Searchlight estimates 500 million-plus sites run WordPress (a self-reported figure).

WordPress.org enabled forced auto-updates for affected supported installs. Cloudflare pushed WAF rules for both CVEs to all plans including free tier, but calls them a stopgap, not a substitute for patching.

watchTowr reports early in-the-wild exploitation after PoCs hit GitHub; some public exploits crack admin hashes via SQLi first, while others claim true pre-auth RCE. BleepingComputer says it has asked Searchlight to confirm no admin password is required.

Interim mitigations: block /wp-json/batch/v1 and ?rest_route=/batch/v1 at the WAF, or disable anonymous REST API access. Searchlight is withholding technical details and offers wp2shell.com to test exposure.
summary: Researchers at Searchlight Cyber (Adam Kues) disclosed "wp2shell," a chain of two WordPress Core flaws that together enable pre-authentication remote code execution: CVE-2026-63030, a REST API batch-route confusion bug introduced in 6.9, and CVE-2026-60137, a SQL injection in the WP_Query author__not_in parameter. The full RCE chain affects WordPress 6.9.0-6.9.4 and 7.0.0-7.0.1 and is fixed in 6.9.5 and 7.0.2; the SQLi alone reaches back to 6.8.0-6.8.5 but cannot chain to RCE without the 6.9 batch-route bug. Searchlight says the attack works on a default install with no plugins and no preconditions, prompting WordPress.org to enable forced auto-updates. Public PoC exploits have appeared on GitHub, and watchTowr reports the first in-the-wild exploitation; some PoCs crack admin hashes via SQLi while others claim direct pre-auth RCE. Cloudflare deployed WAF rules blocking both CVEs across all plans; recommended interim mitigations include blocking the /wp-json/batch/v1 and ?rest_route=/batch/v1 endpoints or disabling anonymous REST API access, though vendors stress these are temporary and not a substitute for patching.
novelty: public PoCs and in-the-wild exploitation confirmed for wp2shell chain
audience: WordPress site admins and security teams managing web infrastructure

title: Python 3.15's JIT is now back on track
why: After a year where the CPython JIT was often slower than the interpreter and its sponsor pulled funding, a volunteer team now reports the 3.15 alpha JIT beating the interpreter by 5-12%, so JIT-on-by-default in a future CPython is again plausible.
notes: Reported speedups (geometric means, preliminary): 11-12% faster than the tail-calling interpreter on macOS AArch64, 5-6% faster than the standard interpreter on x86_64 Linux; per-benchmark results range from ~20% slowdown to over 100% speedup, excluding the unpack_sequence microbenchmark. These are self-reported numbers from JIT contributor Ken Jin.

The Faster CPython team lost its main corporate sponsor in 2025; the project shifted to community stewardship with a stated plan (set at the Cambridge core sprint) of a 5% faster JIT by 3.15 and 10% by 3.16, plus reducing bus factor to two active maintainers per stage (frontend/middle-end/backend).

Middle-end recurring contributors grew from 2 to 4 by splitting work into small, immediately actionable issues; 11 contributors worked on converting nearly the whole interpreter into a JIT-optimizer-friendly form.

A tracing-JIT frontend rewrite is credited as the biggest win: 'dual dispatch' uses a single tracing instruction shared across a second dispatch table instead of duplicating instructions, avoiding interpreter/code bloat; it went from ~6% slower to ~1.x% faster and increased JIT code coverage by ~50%.

Reference-count elimination removed a per-decrement branch left in JITted code, and served as the main parallelizable task to onboard contributors; trace recording is estimated at 3-5x slower than the specializing interpreter.

No free-threading support yet (targeted for 3.15/3.16); infrastructure is 4 machines in a contributor's closet running daily benchmark runs. Author repeatedly attributes results to luck and does not provide full benchmark methodology.
summary: Ken Jin reports that the CPython JIT, which in 3.13/3.14 was frequently slower than the interpreter, now hits its performance goals early in the 3.15 alpha: 11-12% faster than the tail-calling interpreter on macOS AArch64 and 5-6% faster than the standard interpreter on x86_64 Linux, by preliminary geometric means, with per-benchmark results spanning a 20% slowdown to over 100% speedup. The turnaround followed the Faster CPython team losing its main sponsor in 2025 and moving to volunteer community stewardship, with a plan for 5% by 3.15 and 10% by 3.16. Technical gains came mainly from rewriting the frontend into a tracing JIT using a 'dual dispatch' scheme, one shared tracing instruction plus a second dispatch table, which avoided code bloat and raised JIT coverage by about 50%, plus reference-count branch elimination that also served to onboard new contributors. The team grew middle-end maintainers from 2 to 4 by decomposing the work into small tasks, and relies on daily benchmark runs on four machines. Free-threading support is not yet implemented and is targeted for 3.15/3.16. The numbers are self-reported and preliminary, and the post does not include full benchmark methodology.
novelty: community-run CPython JIT hits perf goals early via tracing rewrite
audience: Python core devs and performance engineers tracking CPython internals

title: AI Mania Is Eviscerating Global Decision-Making
why: If you are being measured on token spend or asked to "AI-wash" work you did by hand, this essay names the incentive trap and argues from consulting fieldwork that the productivity numbers your leadership repeats are, as a rule, fabricated.
notes: Nikhil Suresh (Ludicity) writes from consulting experience: ~300 professional catchups and lead technical work on most of his firm's engagements. He claims a 0% success rate across every AI project his team observed over 18 months, including projects they weren't hired for. This is self-reported anecdote, not audited data, and 'failure' is his own judgment.

Argued failure modes: companies are already bad at shipping software, and AI adds a novelty risk on top; internal chatbots see little uptake because an LLM can only surface what is documented, and documentation is poor. Named anecdote: a Mitsubishi voice bot promised a callback after an auto failure and never delivered, logging no error, so the miss looks like a resolved query.

Claims a coordination problem keeps executives lying: if a vendor exec calls a customer's '100x productivity' claim implausible, it reads as heresy and risks contract cancellation, so honesty gets you fired and everyone points guns at each other. Cites an anonymous career CISO saying most CISOs are quietly skeptical but afraid to speak.

Describes 'AI-washing': engineers do work the normal way then credit Claude, and 'token leaderboards' reward higher spend, so optimizers run LLMs in plausible loops and ignore the output to hit quotas. Claims the only people fired over this are those who voiced doubt.

On demos: Snowflake's Cortex text-to-SQL was pitched by Snowflake staff at ~92% accuracy in ideal config (one in ten numbers wrong for a CFO); the author says lukewarm clients flipped to buying frenzy on seeing it, so his team pulled Cortex from demos and drops sales where clients show more than fleeting AI interest.

Structural claim: many 'AI projects' are ordinary projects with an AI element bolted on to pass a purity test, e.g. an Oracle-to-Snowflake migration billed as AI-driven after the LLM step failed and translation was done by hand. Note the framing is opinion cross-posted to a company blog for perceived authority.
summary: In this opinion essay, consultant Nikhil Suresh (Ludicity) argues that corporate AI mandates have broken organizational decision-making, drawing on roughly 300 professional catchups and his firm's engagements. He claims a 0% success rate across all AI projects his team observed over 18 months and asserts that nearly every corporate report of 'massive AI productivity gains' is untrue, because boards, executives, employees, vendors, and consultants all have incentives to obfuscate outcomes. He describes common failures (internal chatbots undermined by poor documentation, a Mitsubishi bot that never called back yet logged no error), employee coping tactics (AI-washing real work and gaming 'token leaderboards'), and a game-theoretic bind where executives dare not contradict customers' inflated claims for fear of losing enterprise contracts. He recounts pulling Snowflake's Cortex (cited by Snowflake staff at ~92% accuracy) from demos after lukewarm clients turned into compulsive buyers, and argues many 'AI projects' are conventional work with an AI veneer added to pass an ideological purity test. The piece is self-reported anecdote and argument rather than measured evidence, and identifying details are withheld.
novelty: consultant claims 0% success rate across all observed AI projects in 18 months
audience: engineers/managers skeptical of enterprise AI hype and executive incentive distortion

title: Speech Recognition and TTS in less than 500kb
why: A full offline voice loop, wake-word to spoken reply, now fits on an 80-cent RP2350 with no cloud, no phone, and no network dependency, making voice control viable on the cheapest microcontrollers.
notes: Reference platform is the Raspberry Pi RP2350 (~$0.80 retail, 520 KiB SRAM); the demo pipeline provisions ~468 KiB SRAM and ~3.6 MiB flash, including the embedded neural voice pack.

Three stages: TinyVadCNN VAD (~89 KiB flash, ~36 KiB SRAM, ~25 MMAC/s), SpellingCNN STT (~1.3 MiB flash, ~346 KiB SRAM, ~36 MMAC/s), and a neural diphone TTS at 16 kHz (~1.8 MiB voice pack, ~340 KiB SRAM, ~65 MMAC/s peak).

SRAM is not additive: VAD, STT, and TTS run sequentially and time-share one ~384 KiB TensorFlow Lite Micro arena, which is how the total stays under 500 KiB. Flash figures are .text+.rodata measured with arm-none-eabi-size.
End-to-end latency to classify and speak a reply is ~0.7-1.0 s on the RP2350 demo (self-reported by the project, no external benchmark methodology given).
MIT licensed and marketed as suitable for commercial use; VAD, STT, and TTS libraries can be used independently. Ships a complete example that configures wifi on the MCU by voice.
Numbers are vendor demo figures for one board; the article gives no accuracy/WER data, vocabulary size limits, or noise-robustness details for the recognizer.
summary: Moonshine Micro is an open-source (MIT) toolkit from Moonshine AI that runs a complete on-device voice pipeline, voice-activity detection, speech-to-text, custom word recognition, and neural text-to-speech, on microcontrollers and DSPs, using the 80-cent Raspberry Pi RP2350 as its reference platform. The demo pipeline fits in ~468 KiB of SRAM on the 520 KiB chip and ~3.6 MiB of flash, with the key trick being that the three neural stages run sequentially and share a single ~384 KiB TensorFlow Lite Micro arena rather than each allocating its own. Reported per-stage costs are ~25 MMAC/s for VAD, ~36 MMAC/s for the SpellingCNN STT, and up to ~65 MMAC/s for a 16 kHz neural diphone TTS, with classify-and-speak latency of roughly 0.7-1.0 s. The VAD, STT, and TTS libraries can be used independently and rely on the bundled TFLM library for inference. A full example demonstrates configuring wifi on the MCU entirely by voice. All figures are project-reported for the RP2350 demo; the article provides no recognition-accuracy or noise-robustness data.
novelty: full voice pipeline (VAD+STT+TTS) under 500KB RAM on 80-cent MCU
audience: embedded/IoT engineers building offline voice interfaces

title: Battery packs: Let's talk about crates, baby
why: Picking crates from crates.io is the friction point new Rust adopters complain about most; a working `cargo-bp` prototype turns curated crate sets into installable, forkable crates you can try today.
notes: Battery packs are ordinary crates named X-battery-pack: the pack's Cargo.toml dependencies are the recommendations, features map to common co-use groupings, and examples act as templates. You depend on the underlying crates directly, not the pack, so pack updates never break your build.

Prototype ships now: `cargo install cargo-bp`, then `cargo bp list` (populated via a crates.io search), `cargo bp add cli`, and `cargo bp add embedded`. `cargo bp new battery-pack` scaffolds a new pack from a bundled template.

Selection UI supports grouped categories with 'pick at most one' vs 'pick any number' semantics, demonstrated by an embedded-battery-pack derived from the Awesome Embedded Rust repo (concurrency framework: embassy or rtic; HALs for esp32, nrf52840, rp2040, stm32f0, etc.).

'Batteries' extend beyond dependencies to recipes and templates: the CI battery pack (credited to Jess Izen) scaffolds GitHub Actions via `cargo bp add ci`, using a minijinja-based template system the author expects to expand.

Proposed governance layer: Rust Commercial Network (RCN)-sponsored packs would each carry an 'ecosystem fund' where sponsors pay for maintainer support, early patches, and security disclosures, and could serve as a neutral home for cross-runtime async interop traits (tokio vs in-house runtimes).

This is a proposal and prototype, not shipped Rust tooling; the author (babysteps/Niko Matsakis) frames it as one candidate for crates.io's next 'layer of structure' and explicitly invites alternatives. Acknowledged risk: pack proliferation could reduce rather than aid discovery.
summary: Battery packs are a proposed curation layer for Rust's crate ecosystem: a curated, themed set of crates (CLI, backend web service, embedded, error handling, CI) packaged and distributed as an ordinary crate named X-battery-pack, where the pack's dependencies are the recommendations. A live prototype exists via `cargo install cargo-bp`, with commands like `cargo bp list`, `cargo bp add cli`, and `cargo bp add embedded`; the embedded pack (derived from Awesome Embedded Rust) shows a grouped selection UI distinguishing 'pick at most one' from 'pick any number.' Batteries can also be recipes or templates, such as a CI pack that scaffolds GitHub Actions using a minijinja template system. Because users depend on the underlying crates rather than the pack itself, pack updates do not break existing code, and anyone can publish a pack, which the author argues addresses the 'who decides?' problem better than a centralized extended standard library. The author further envisions Rust Commercial Network-sponsored packs carrying ecosystem funds to pay maintainers and offering a neutral home for cross-runtime interop. It is framed as an open proposal, with the acknowledged risk that too many packs could hurt discoverability.
novelty: Working prototype of a user-publishable crate curation layer for Rust — battery packs are themselves crates (named X-battery-pack) distributed via crates.io, not a centralized standard library extension
audience: Rust practitioners tired of crate discovery overhead, and Rust ecosystem working group contributors

title: Regressive JPEGs
why: You can smuggle a playing video, or a stateful single-page app, into one standards-compliant JPEG with no JavaScript or CSS, using only how browsers render progressive scans over a slow connection.
notes: Progressive JPEGs split compressed data into multiple scans, each with its own header setting channels, DCT spectral (bin) range, and precision; a partial download renders at reduced quality instead of cutting off.

Concatenating same-resolution images and stripping the start-of-image, start-of-frame, and end-of-image markers makes a decoder redraw successive full frames as bytes arrive; the author did this with a short C program.
Decoders cap total scans (to avoid zip-bomb-style unbounded decode), so naive concatenation tops out near 9 frames.

A progressive scan cannot mix DC (bin 0) and AC (bins above 0) data; the smallest legal frame is a single DC-only scan, which is standards-compliant and generated via jpegtran with a custom .scans file.
DC-only frames render at 1/16 original resolution because the DCT works on 16x16 blocks, and using only DC scans avoids the ghosting that AC refinement scans cause.

Chrome renders roughly 90 frames before giving up, Firefox more; 90-scan files work almost everywhere. There is no timing metadata, so playback speed depends entirely on network delay, and the author calls it impractical beyond trolling.
summary: The article shows that progressive JPEG's per-scan structure, where each scan header declares its own channels, DCT bin range, and precision, can be abused to make a single image file play as an animation. Concatenating multiple same-resolution JPEGs and removing the SOI, SOF, and EOI markers causes a decoder streaming over a slow network to render each embedded image in turn. Decoders cap scan counts (to prevent zip-bomb-like decode blowups), limiting naive concatenation to about 9 frames, so the author minimizes scans per frame using DC-only scans, which are standards-compliant, avoid AC-refinement ghosting, and render at 1/16 resolution because the DCT operates on 16x16 blocks. This gets roughly 90 frames in Chrome and more in Firefox. Frames are produced with jpegtran and a custom .scans file; demos include a Bad Apple video rendered with HTML dialog tags and an interactive single-page app with no CSS or JavaScript. The author notes the technique has no timing control, so playback depends on network delay, and treats it as a curiosity rather than a practical format.
novelty: Using JPEG progressive scan concatenation with DC-only scans as a standards-compliant multi-frame animation format inside a single image file
audience: Systems/format-curious engineers interested in compression internals and creative format abuse

title: Ring-Zero: Scaling Zero RL to a Trillion Parameters for Emergent Reasoning
why: This is the first published account of what zero-RL training dynamics and emergent reasoning behaviors look like at 1 trillion parameters, a scale most reinforcement-learning studies have never touched.
notes: Ring-2.5-1T-Zero is trained with 'zero RL': reinforcement learning on verifiable rewards only, no human-annotated data, to elicit chain-of-thought reasoning at 1T parameters.
Stability at scale is attributed to three fixes: clipped importance sampling, training-inference ratio correction, and mixed-precision control; the abstract names them but gives no ablation numbers.

Training is described as two sequential phases, an initial discovery phase followed by a sharpening phase, framed as validating the scaling 'bitter lesson' for sample efficiency and performance ceilings.
Five behaviors are said to emerge spontaneously, making hand-crafted heuristics redundant: anthropomorphism, structured formatting, self-verification, parallel reasoning, and 'context anxiety'.
Evaluated on seven mathematical benchmarks with only a self-reported 'competitive performance' claim; no specific scores, baselines, or comparison models appear in the abstract.

Proposes a CoT-quality framework scoring reasoning traces on comprehensibility, reproducibility, and efficiency rather than final-answer correctness, where the authors report an advantage; methodology is not detailed here.
summary: Ring-Zero reports a zero-RL training run, reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards and no human annotation, scaled to a 1-trillion-parameter model called Ring-2.5-1T-Zero. The authors describe a stable pipeline built on clipped importance sampling, training-inference ratio correction, and mixed-precision control, and claim that naive scaling otherwise produces poor readability, token redundancy, and inconsistent reasoning depth. They report three findings: scaling to 1T improves sample efficiency and performance ceilings; training moves through a discovery phase then a sharpening phase; and the model spontaneously develops behaviors including anthropomorphism, structured formatting, self-verification, parallel reasoning, and 'context anxiety'. Evaluation covers seven mathematical benchmarks with a self-reported 'competitive' result but no cited numbers or baselines in the abstract. The paper also proposes a chain-of-thought quality framework rating traces on comprehensibility, reproducibility, and efficiency, where the authors claim an edge. All performance claims here are self-reported and lack methodology in the abstract text.
novelty: First documented zero RL training run (no human-annotated data, verifiable rewards only) at 1-trillion-parameter scale, with empirical characterization of emergent behaviors and training dynamics at that scale
audience: ML researchers and engineers working on reasoning models, RLVR/GRPO training pipelines, or large-scale LLM post-training

title: Static search trees: 40x faster than binary search (2024)
why: If you index sorted 32-bit data (suffix arrays, lookup tables) for throughput, this shows how to beat std binary search by a claimed 40x on out-of-cache data using an S+ tree, query batching, and hand-written AVX2, with the assembly diff for every instruction saved.
notes: Baseline chain: Eytzinger layout is ~4x faster than std binary search once data exceeds L3, because it can prefetch 4 cache lines ahead; the S+ tree adds cache-line-dense nodes on top for the claimed 40x. All numbers are self-benchmarked on an i7-10750H pinned to 2.6GHz (32KiB L1, 256KiB L2, 12MiB L3), AVX2 only, no AVX-512, hyper-threading off.

Node = 16 u32 values = 64 bytes = one cache line, giving branching factor 17; using S+ (values duplicated into internal nodes, every query terminates at the same leaf depth) is what makes batching clean, since every query in a batch takes the same number of steps.

Batching is the main throughput win: it saturates around 12 in-flight reads, matching the CPU's 12 line fill buffers, cutting amortized RAM wait from ~80ns latency to ~8ns; 1->2 gives sub-linear gain because out-of-order execution already overlaps ~2 queries. Author ships batch size 128 for cleaner plots and slightly lower per-batch overhead.

find is optimized from a branchy linear scan (branch predictor stalls on the early return) to branchless count-of-lesser, then to manual AVX2 using vpackssdw + vpmovmskb read on 16-bit output (taking MSB and middle bit, dividing count by 2), eliminating a stray vpshufd and saving 3 instructions versus the auto-vectorized popcount version.

Byte-based pointers pre-multiply child indices by 64 so the loop drops two shlq $6 shifts the compiler emits for 64-byte stride, cutting the inner loop from 17 to 15 instructions; a further /2*64 => *32 rewrite removes a provably-useless andl $-64 mask.

2MB hugepages cut TLB pressure, but transparent hugepages only trigger on allocations that actually leave the heap, so the tree is rounded up to 32MB; the benefit only shows for datasets roughly 1MB-32MB. Input assumed static (fixed reference genome), values constrained to 31 bits so signed SIMD compares work. Latency jumps in plots line up with new tree layers at powers of 17.
summary: A detailed engineering writeup that implements and optimizes a static search tree (S+ tree) in Rust for high-throughput queries over a sorted array of 32-bit unsigned integers, returning the smallest element >= q. Starting from Algorithmica's S+ tree baseline, the author reports reaching roughly 40x the throughput of the Rust standard library binary search on large out-of-cache inputs, with the Eytzinger layout alone giving about 4x. The core techniques are: 64-byte cache-line nodes of 16 u32 (branching factor 17), query batching that exploits the CPU's ~12 line fill buffers to overlap many RAM reads, explicit prefetching of the next child node, and hand-written AVX2 that trims the per-query find down to a vpackssdw + vpmovmskb sequence. Further gains come from byte-based pointer arithmetic that eliminates shift instructions for the 64-byte stride (17->15 instructions per iteration) and from 2MB hugepages to reduce TLB misses. All measurements are self-reported on the author's i7-10750H laptop with AVX2 only and frequency fixed at 2.6GHz; methodology (median of 5, hugepage rounding to 32MB, 31-bit value assumption) is described, but results are not independently verified. The stated motivation is speeding up suffix-array search for bioinformatics indexing of static reference genomes.
novelty: Concrete implementation of batching + manual AVX2 SIMD + byte-based pointer arithmetic on top of Algorithmica's S+ tree baseline, with assembly-level justification for each optimization step, reaching 40x over std binary search
audience: Systems programmers and performance engineers who need high-throughput sorted-array lookups (bioinformatics, database indexing, suffix arrays)

title: How Cops Use Flock to Track People, Not Cars
why: The license-plate cameras your town approved to find stolen cars now let any officer run natural-language person searches ("male with brown hair," "gray shirt") across hundreds of camera networks at once, without face recognition and largely without public disclosure.
notes: Flock's FreeForm search, launched February 2025 and marketed around vehicles, lets police query cameras with plain-language phrases; the queries surface in the 'text_prompt' field of 'Network Audit' spreadsheets obtained via public records and aggregated at HaveIBeenFlocked.com.

Documented person searches span dozens to nearly 100 camera networks at once: California Highway Patrol queried 274 cameras for a 'gray shirt'; Texas DPS searched 96 networks for 'man wearing a black t-shirt and shorts'; Milford CT PD searched 100+ cameras for descriptors like 'male with brown hair.'

Flock says FreeForm 'is not facial recognition,' cannot identify a person by name, and has 'guardrails' that block searches on race, ethnicity, religion, or nationality and alert admins when attempted; 404 Media found race-referencing searches (e.g., Atlanta PD 'non caucasion male') that completed anyway.

Searches inferred political affiliation, such as Anne Arundel County MD PD querying 198 networks for 'white jeep with trump flag.' Vague prompts ('person walking,' 'backpack,' 'tall man') can pull up innocent, unrelated people.

The feature ties to Flock's Condor video cameras (with 'people detection alerts' and 'Guardian Mode') but is designed to work alongside its license-plate readers, per a Flock consultant who called combined video-plus-LPR evidence 'game changing' for prosecution.

Prior 404 Media reporting: in May 2025 Flock planned to cross-reference LPR data with broker and breached data to 'jump from LPR to person' (scrapped after coverage and internal pressure); in December 2025 at least 60 Condor cameras were left exposed to the open internet.
summary: 404 Media reviewed public-records data showing US police have used Flock's FreeForm feature hundreds of times to search for specific people by description rather than by license plate. FreeForm, launched in February 2025, accepts natural-language prompts (clothing, hair, tattoos, objects) and applies AI image recognition across camera feeds, sometimes spanning dozens to nearly 100 camera networks and hundreds of cameras in a single query. The data, drawn from 'Network Audit' spreadsheets aggregated at HaveIBeenFlocked.com, includes searches referencing race and apparent political affiliation, some of which Flock says its guardrails should block or flag. Flock states FreeForm is not facial recognition, cannot identify people by name, and is meant for working from witness descriptions, citing AMBER alert and missing-person use cases. Civil-liberties voices from the ACLU, CDT, and EFF argue AI video analytics turns vast footage into a searchable index of ordinary people, sweeping in innocents and expanding surveillance well beyond the stolen-car pitch. The piece situates this within earlier 404 Media reporting on Flock's scrapped plan to link LPR data to breached/broker data and on exposed Condor cameras.
novelty: Primary-source data from public records reveals Flock's "FreeForm" natural-language AI video search is being used at scale for person-tracking across hundreds of cameras simultaneously — a capability not publicly disclosed in Flock's original marketing and underdiscussed in prior coverage
audience: Security-minded engineers and policy-engaged technologists tracking real-world AI surveillance deployment at infrastructure scale

title: Zigzag Decoding with AVX-512
why: If you write AVX-512 intrinsics, this shows Clang will silently canonicalize your hand-tuned mask code back to the baseline while GCC keeps it, and that GFNI's vgf2p8affineqb collapses an entire 8-bit unzigzag into one instruction.
notes: Baseline SSE2/AVX zigzag decode is four instructions (vpsrld/vpandq/vpsubd/vpxorq), 3-cycle latency on Zen 4, ~1 cycle throughput because the ops spread across all four FP ports.

The AVX-512 mask variant (vptestmd plus masked vpxord/vpsub) drops to three instructions but raises latency to 4 cycles, since vptestmd alone is 3-cycle latency per uops.info; masked XOR only works on 32-bit lanes, so 8/16-bit needs a masked subtract of -1 instead.

GFNI's vgf2p8affineqb does an 8x8 GF(2) matrix-vector multiply per byte; the author derives the unzigzag matrix by XOR-ing a right-shift matrix with a low-bit-broadcast matrix, yielding constant 0x0305091121418101 that decodes 8-bit zigzag in one 3-cycle instruction running on two ports at 2/cycle.

Clang canonicalizes the mask intrinsics straight back to the four-instruction baseline, erasing the optimization; GCC preserves it. The author frames this as LLVM canonicalizing vector intrinsics and then optimizing against a model that ignores real hardware bottlenecks.

Measured impact in meshoptimizer: the mask trick gives ~3% in the throughput-bound meshlet codec (which lacks an AVX-512 or even AVX2 path); both tricks are inconclusive in the vertex codec, bottlenecked by a latency-bound delta-accumulation spine (one cycle per add) and store-unit saturation from many narrow stores.

GFNI carries a separate CPUID bit, is excluded from AVX10.2, but ships on all AMD AVX-512 CPUs and modern relevant Intel parts (older Cascade Lake had AVX-512 without GFNI); author notes competing ISAs like SVE2 lack ready equivalents for GF(2) multiply, compress/expand, multishift, and bitshuffle.
summary: Arseny Kapoulkine documents two AVX-512 zigzag-decode optimizations he explored while speeding up meshoptimizer's vertex/meshlet codecs but did not ship. The first replaces the branchless four-instruction decode with AVX-512 predication (vptestmd plus a masked XOR/subtract), cutting to three instructions but adding a cycle of latency on Zen 4; it helps only in throughput-bound loops, where it gained ~3% in the meshlet codec, and Clang defeats it by canonicalizing the intrinsics back to the baseline while GCC keeps it. The second uses GFNI's vgf2p8affineqb, an 8x8 GF(2) matrix multiply, to collapse the full 8-bit unzigzag into one instruction via a derived matrix constant (0x0305091121418101), which is more than twice as good in throughput-bound contexts but limited to byte-width values. Neither moved the needle in the vertex codec because it is bottlenecked elsewhere: a latency-bound delta-accumulation spine and store-unit saturation. The piece doubles as a tutorial on GF(2) affine transforms and a caveat about relying on GFNI (separate CPUID bit, absent from AVX10.2 but present on all AMD AVX-512 parts).
novelty: Reduction of 8-bit zigzag decode to a single vgf2p8affineqb instruction via GF(2) affine matrix derivation; plus documented Clang silently defeating AVX-512 mask optimizations by canonicalizing intrinsics
audience: Systems/performance engineers doing SIMD optimization on x86 CPUs (graphics, codec, compression work)

title: Enterprise Haskell at H-E-B
why: A named retailer documents eight years and ~1M lines of production Haskell, including the two projects where it failed and why, so you can weigh a functional-language bet against real operational war stories instead of advocacy.
notes: H-E-B has run Haskell in production since ~2018, now nearly 1 million lines across multiple teams on supply-chain systems feeding 435+ stores; began as a consultant-pitched replacement for the company's largest COBOL mainframe app.

Two failures reported: one team of seasoned non-Haskell devs was killed by a space leak with no expert to diagnose it (working from a copied project template); another died when the Haskell advocates left. Author's read: teams without genuine buy-in retreat to a Spring Boot escape hatch, now wider because LLMs can mechanically translate whole codebases.

Concrete runtime pain: double-free errors from custom reference-counting logic, and parallel GC permanently disabled on one service to stop a runaway space leak; fixes came from segfault core dumps, GC metrics, and heap profiles. Author claims the GHC runtime was never the root cause of an outage and reports no space-leak fear since info-table profiling landed in GHC 9.2.

"Complexity budget" framing: dependencies spend it for you. Pulling in servant forces API-as-type-level-DSL plus type families; haxl forces modeling every data source as a GADT indexed by result type. Their local convention bans "spooky action at a distance" (no TemplateHaskell, no Generic-derived serialization) in favor of explicit boilerplate.

Integration strategy: hand-written FFI wrappers around simdjson (C++), Oracle ODPI-C, and IBM MQ rather than waiting on upstream SDKs; H-E-B sponsored Debug Adapter Protocol support for Haskell in VS Code. Production stack includes warp, aeson, attoparsec, stm, conduit, servant, orville-postgresql, plus an in-house transactional Postgres job queue (arbiter).

AI angle (author's claim, not measured): as coding agents shift the bottleneck from writing to verifying code, GHC's type system gives agents a tight feedback loop that rejects hallucinated code before it runs; caveat that rote library ports often miss the type system without careful prompting.
summary: A H-E-B engineer recounts eight years of running Haskell in production, now nearly 1 million lines across several teams on critical supply-chain systems, after Haskell was pitched to replace the company's largest COBOL mainframe application. The piece argues Haskell's value for enterprises is fewer classes of runtime failure, painless refactoring during multi-year strangler-fig migrations, strong FFI and parsing for legacy integration, and embedded DSLs (citing Uber-derived Hydra) that generate typed code for non-Haskell teams. It is unusually candid about failure: two projects died, one to a space leak on a team with no Haskell expert and one to advocate departures, and the author frames team passion, not language quality, as the real differentiator now that LLMs make any codebase easy to rewrite. Operational war stories include double-free errors, disabling parallel GC to stop a space leak, and reliance on core dumps and heap profiles, though the author says the GHC runtime never caused an outage and incidents trace to business logic more than types or runtime. It tracks how tooling matured from GHC 8.2 (no HLS, nascent GHCup) to today's HLS, hs-opentelemetry, and info-table profiling. The author closes by positioning GHC's type checker as a fast feedback loop for verifying agent-written code. All performance and reliability claims are self-reported.
novelty: First detailed, named-company retrospective on running ~1M LOC Haskell in production at a major US retailer across 8 years, including specific failure modes and cultural lessons that failed projects don't usually publish
audience: Software engineers evaluating functional/typed languages for production systems or managing large-scale legacy migrations

title: Moroccan intelligence insider reveals widespread use of Pegasus hacking software
why: Insider testimony from inside Morocco's DGST turns the Pegasus Project from database inference into operational detail: how the spyware was acquired, when it was deployed, and against whom, including a sitting NATO-ally prime minister and defense minister.
notes: A whistleblower codenamed 'Safir,' who worked for Morocco's Direction Générale de la Surveillance du Territoire (DGST) for about a decade, says the service adopted Pegasus in 2017 after an NSO Group demonstration at a Rabat property nicknamed the 'FSSYS villa,' after FSSYS Maroc, then the Moroccan branch of UAE surveillance intermediary al-Fahad; two other former agents corroborated, and Amnesty International's Security Lab did forensic analysis. Investigation coordinated by Forbidden Stories across 14 outlets.

Safir claims the UAE bought Pegasus and gave it to Morocco free, likening it to a shared Netflix subscription: 'The Emirates bought it and redistributed it to friendly services.' Stated internal doctrine reserved Pegasus for high-value targets after cheaper methods (internet-café terminals, shopkeepers selling pre-infected handsets): 'We never start with Pegasus. It's the monster's weapon.'

Targeting began September 2017 with four test Moroccan numbers (two belonging to DGST staff), then journalists and human rights defenders, then foreign targets. Aminatou Haidar's phone showed Pegasus traces dating to 2018 and again November 2021; over 200 Spanish numbers were selected.

Spain confirmed in May 2022 that PM Pedro Sánchez and Defense Minister Margarita Robles were infected in May/June 2021; Interior Minister Grande-Marlaska and Agriculture Minister Planas were also targeted. Court material shows one Morocco-assigned attacker account used against French targets also hit Robles and Grande-Marlaska. The Spanish judicial inquiry was shelved again in January 2026, citing lack of cooperation from Israeli authorities.

Morocco's NSO end-user codename 'Morgan' was confirmed by former NSO employees; the naming scheme pairs a country's first letter with a car brand (Saudi Arabia = 'Subaru'), drawn from an unsealed August 2018 Q Cyber Technologies board presentation surfaced via Meta's US suit against NSO.

NSO was placed on the US Commerce blacklist in November 2021; three weeks later Israel's defense ministry reportedly barred cyber-tech exports to countries including Morocco and the UAE. The investigation found no forensic evidence of Pegasus use in Morocco after late 2021. NSO, Morocco, the UAE, al-Fahad's parent, and Spanish ministries were approached for comment.
summary: A former DGST agent using the pseudonym 'Safir' has given the first insider account of how Morocco deployed NSO Group's Pegasus spyware, corroborated by two other ex-agents, leaked material, and Amnesty International Security Lab forensics, as part of a 14-outlet investigation coordinated by Forbidden Stories. Safir says Morocco adopted Pegasus in 2017 following an NSO demonstration at a Rabat villa tied to UAE intermediary al-Fahad, and that the UAE supplied the tool for free on a shared-account basis. According to the source, Pegasus was reserved for high-value targets after cheaper methods were exhausted, but targeting records show more than 200 Spanish numbers selected, plus journalists and human rights defenders; Spain confirmed in 2022 that PM Sánchez and Defense Minister Robles were infected in 2021, and court material links a Morocco-assigned attacker account to targeting of Spanish ministers. Former NSO employees confirmed Morocco's end-user codename as 'Morgan,' consistent with a naming scheme (country initial plus car brand) exposed in an unsealed 2018 Q Cyber board presentation. NSO was blacklisted by the US in November 2021, Israel subsequently restricted exports to Morocco and the UAE, and no Pegasus use in Morocco was found after late 2021. Morocco has long denied using Pegasus against critics, and NSO, Moroccan, UAE, and Spanish authorities were approached for comment.
novelty: First insider whistleblower testimony from within Morocco's DGST confirming Pegasus operational details, including UAE gift mechanism and internal targeting doctrine — goes beyond prior Pegasus Project database analysis
audience: Security researchers and policy-focused engineers tracking nation-state spyware operations and NSO Group's client network

title: kernel asynchronous reads in PostgreSQL 19 (io_uring)
why: PostgreSQL 19 lets a backend push reads straight to the kernel via io_uring, keeping ~30 I/Os in flight without dedicated worker processes; the tradeoff is a monitoring trap where load average hits 25 while CPUs sit 95% in iowait.
notes: PostgreSQL 19beta1 exposes io_method=io_uring; inside Docker seccomp blocks the io_uring syscalls, so the author runs with --security-opt seccomp=unconfined. Defaults observed: io_combine_limit=128kB, io_max_concurrency=64, io_max_workers=8.

strace shows the submit/reap split: io_uring_enter(fd,1,0,0) enqueues one request without blocking, then io_uring_enter(fd,0,1,IORING_ENTER_GETEVENTS) waits for a completion. The worker method instead issues synchronous pread64()/preadv() from separate 'postgres: io worker' processes that can block in D-state.

io_combine (merging nearby block reads into larger ops, visible as prefetch) is identical for worker and io_uring; only submission/completion differs. On 2-worker cold-cache scan: 8,247 I/O ops, just 54 waits, prefetch avg ~74 against capacity 94, ~943ms.

Scaling to 5 parallel workers pushed the same scan to 48,296ms with 8,429 of 8,661 ops waiting; ~5 I/Os in flight per process (~30 total). Author reads this as storage-bound, not CPU-bound: load average 25.11 with %Cpu 95.3 wa and ~4% active. These are single self-reported runs, no methodology on repeats or storage type (iSCSI/XFS visible in top).

New wait events: AioIoSubmission (fast, io_uring only) and AioIoCompletion (both methods). The stated gotcha: Linux load average counts uninterruptible-sleep tasks, so io_uring can inflate load average with few visible R or D processes, complicating diagnosis.
Async I/O only helps where PostgreSQL can predict page accesses: Sequential Scan, Bitmap Heap Scan, Vacuum. Reads of large TOASTed values still fall back to synchronous DataFileRead.
summary: Franck Pachot traces PostgreSQL 19beta1 running a parallel sequential scan under io_method=io_uring versus io_method=worker, using strace and EXPLAIN (analyze, buffers, io). With io_uring the backend submits reads directly to the kernel submission queue via io_uring_enter(fd,1,0,0) and reaps them via io_uring_enter(fd,0,1,IORING_ENTER_GETEVENTS), keeping multiple reads in flight instead of delegating blocking pread64()/preadv() calls to io worker processes. io_combine (read merging) is unchanged across methods; only how I/O is submitted and completed differs. A cold-cache 2-worker scan showed 8,247 I/O ops with only 54 waits and prefetch averaging ~74 of capacity 94. Raising to 5 workers produced 48,296ms with 8,429/8,661 waits, a load average of 25.11, and 95.3% iowait against ~4% active CPU, which the author reads as storage-bound. He flags a monitoring hazard: io_uring can inflate Linux load average without obvious runnable (R) or uninterruptible (D) tasks, and notes new wait events AioIoSubmission and AioIoCompletion, while async I/O applies only to predictable access patterns (Seq Scan, Bitmap Heap Scan, Vacuum).
novelty: Hands-on strace + EXPLAIN ANALYZE traces of PostgreSQL 19beta1 actually using io_uring syscalls for buffered reads, including a concrete load-average inflation gotcha unique to io_uring's D-state accounting
audience: PostgreSQL DBAs and Linux systems engineers evaluating async-IO backends for read-heavy workloads

title: Pact: Anonymous Credentials for the Web
why: If PACT ships, sites could rate-limit VPN users, private browsers, and AI agents by a scarce signal instead of demanding an email login or blocking them outright, without the hardware attestation lock-in of Apple's Private Access Tokens or Google's abandoned Web Environment Integrity.
notes: Three roles: Anchors issue Privacy Pass-style Endorsement tokens off scarce signals (VPN subscription, verified phone, account in good standing); Moderators verify Endorsements and issue stateful Credentials; each site is bound to a single Moderator so the Moderator set can't become a cross-site fingerprint. In the common case the site is its own Moderator.

Anonymous Credit Tokens replace Privacy Pass's static token buckets with a Credential holding an internal counter the Moderator can raise or lower per observed behavior, without learning the counter value or linking successive presentations; revocation is just refusing to return an updated Credential.

Zero-knowledge issuer blinding lets a Moderator learn only that an Endorsement came from one of its trusted Anchors, not which one. Cost: initial Credential access must be uniform across the Anchor pool, effectively set at the weakest Anchor, with more access earned only through later behavior.

Prio-style multiparty computation gives Moderators aggregate per-Anchor quality scores (from encrypted shares submitted at presentation) to detect bad issuers without exposing individual users. Mozilla frames the stated goal as leaking no more than a single bit: whether the user is below the site's rate limit.

Contrast drawn with device attestation: Google's Web Environment Integrity was abandoned in 2023; Apple's Private Access Tokens (deployed 2022, also built on IETF Privacy Pass) require hardware attestation and, per the article, can't admit other scarcity sources without collusion breaking privacy.

Status is a proposal, not a deployment: architecture sketched at a May 2026 W3C workshop with Cloudflare and Chrome; draft specs headed to IETF (crypto layer) and W3C (WebAPI); author states the full system still needs rigorous privacy and security analysis.
summary: Mozilla's Dennis Jackson proposes PACT (Private Access Control Tokens), a scheme to let websites rate-limit visitors, including VPN users, privacy browsers, and AI agents, by anchoring limits to a scarce signal rather than to identity or hardware attestation. It defines three roles: Anchors issue unlinkable Privacy Pass-style Endorsements to users who hold something scarce (a subscription, verified phone, account in good standing); Moderators verify Endorsements and issue stateful Credentials; and sites each pick one Moderator. Anonymous Credit Tokens give Credentials an internal counter a Moderator can adjust by observed behavior without reading or linking it, while zero-knowledge issuer blinding hides which Anchor backed a Credential (at the cost of uniform initial access set at the weakest Anchor). Prio-style multiparty computation lets Moderators score Anchor quality in aggregate without exposing individuals. The design is positioned as an open, hardware-agnostic alternative to Google's abandoned Web Environment Integrity and Apple's attestation-based Private Access Tokens, aiming to leak at most one bit: whether a user is under the rate limit. It remains an early proposal from a May 2026 W3C workshop with Cloudflare and Chrome, with draft specs headed to IETF and W3C and full security analysis still to come.
novelty: Three-tier PACT architecture (Anchors/Moderators/stateful ACT Credentials) combining Privacy Pass, ZKP issuer blinding, and Prio MPC as an open, hardware-agnostic alternative to device attestation for web rate limiting
audience: Security and web platform engineers working on anti-abuse, anonymous credentials, or privacy-preserving authentication systems

title: Kaiser nurses say AI, workplace surveillance are making their jobs, care worse
why: If you build or defend algorithmic management systems, this is the reference case for how tone-scoring and call-time metrics collide with a licensed profession, a labor contract, and a state legislature at California's largest private employer.
notes: Kaiser piloted an AI tool in summer 2024 that scored empathy and tone of voice in nurse and patient audio; nurses petitioned ('Trust nurses, not AI'), the pilot ended November 2024, and union reps were told it may return. Kaiser declined to answer questions about the tool.

Seven current and former advice/triage nurses describe an informal 15-minute call ceiling that feeds monthly performance scores; exceeding it triggers evaluation meetings even when all clinical duties were done correctly. Kaiser's spokesperson denies using 'Average Handle Time' to assess performance. Interpreter-assisted calls often run 30+ minutes; post-call charting time reportedly dropped from ~10 minutes to 30 seconds or less when lines are busy.

Named Kaiser AI systems: Preventus (discharge timing), Abridge (real-time speech-to-text in in-person visits), EHR-based adverse-event risk scoring, and Bay Area remote patient monitoring pilots. Kaiser declined to share a comprehensive inventory, citing 'security and operational reasons.'

A 2024 National Nurses United survey of 2,000+ nurses: half say their employer runs algorithmic systems on health records; two-thirds say their own assessment disagreed with a computer prediction at least once; 60% distrust their employer to prioritize patient safety when using AI.

Legislative state: Newsom vetoed SB 7 (worker notification before AI-based discipline/evaluation); AB 1018 failed a third year against opposition that included Kaiser; SB 947 (SB 7 successor) plus a bill barring AI emotion prediction of employees and one shielding clinicians who override automated recommendations are pending in 2026.

Evidence gaps and context: a 2024 CalMatters public-records request found no patient complaints against Kaiser over call times, and nurses concede they can't confirm adverse outcomes since contact ends at hangup. Separately, Kaiser paid a $50M CA Department of Managed Health Care fine over delayed behavioral-health appointments and over-routing patients to group therapy.
summary: CalMatters interviewed seven current and former Kaiser Permanente call-center nurses who say workplace surveillance and AI are degrading both their jobs and patient care. Nurses report an informal 15-minute call limit that feeds monthly performance scores and triggers evaluation meetings, plus a suspended 2024 pilot of an AI tool that scored empathy and tone of voice; Kaiser denies using average handle time and declined to discuss specific systems. The company runs AI across care delivery, including Preventus for discharge timing, Abridge for visit transcription, and EHR-based risk scoring, but would not provide an inventory. The dispute is live: the California Nurses Association is negotiating a new contract for 25,000 nurses this month, mental health workers are separately bargaining over AI, and California lawmakers are weighing several bills after Newsom vetoed SB 7 and AB 1018 repeatedly failed. Direct patient harm is unproven, a 2024 records request found no call-time complaints and nurses can't track outcomes after hangup, but cited research links call-center surveillance to higher stress and emotional exhaustion. Kaiser, California's largest private employer serving 9 million state residents, says it deploys AI with human oversight and patient safety in mind.
novelty: Detailed investigative account of Kaiser's specific AI deployments — tone/empathy scoring, Preventus discharge AI, productivity prediction — plus the California legislative fight over workplace AI that Newsom twice blocked
audience: Engineers and policy-watchers tracking real-world consequences of algorithmic management and AI governance in high-stakes sectors

title: First atmosphere found on Earth-like planet in habitable zone of distant star
why: A category that had zero confirmed members now has one: LHS 1140 b is the first rocky planet in a star's habitable zone with a detected atmosphere, moving the "is there life elsewhere" question from candidates to a confirmed detection.
notes: The planet, LHS 1140 b, sits 48 light-years away orbiting a red dwarf smaller and cooler than the Sun; results published in Science, lead author Dr. Collin Cherubim (Harvard), co-author Dr. David Charbonneau.
The only gas detected so far is helium, likely in the upper atmosphere, which cannot support life on its own; the authors note other gases may exist lower down but are not yet detected.

The researchers explicitly state they have not found life; the claim is narrowly an atmosphere on a rocky, habitable-zone world, described by Cherubim as 'the first time anyone has found an atmosphere on a rocky planet in the habitable zone of another star.'
Context on the field's caution: a 2025 NASA-led reanalysis found K2-18b's dimethyl sulphide signal too weak to confirm and showed the gas can form without biology.

JWST ruled out an Earth-like atmosphere on TRAPPIST-1d; TRAPPIST-1e data remain inconclusive. Of 6,000+ known exoplanets, only a few dozen rocky worlds sit in habitable zones, and none had a confirmed atmosphere before this.
summary: Researchers report in Science the first confirmed atmosphere on a rocky, Earth-sized planet inside its star's habitable zone: LHS 1140 b, 48 light-years away, orbiting a small, cool red dwarf. The detected gas is helium, probably in the upper atmosphere, which cannot support life by itself; the authors say other, more life-relevant gases may exist lower down but have not been found. Lead author Dr. Collin Cherubim and co-author Dr. David Charbonneau (both Harvard) frame the result as evidence that Earth-like conditions could exist beyond the solar system, while stating clearly that no life has been detected. The article situates the finding against prior candidates: a 2025 NASA-led reanalysis found K2-18b's dimethyl sulphide signal too weak to confirm and abiotically producible, and JWST ruled out an Earth-like atmosphere on TRAPPIST-1d with TRAPPIST-1e inconclusive. Of more than 6,000 known exoplanets, only a few dozen are small, rocky, and in habitable zones, and none had a confirmed atmosphere until this detection.
novelty: First confirmed atmospheric detection on a rocky exoplanet inside a stellar habitable zone — a category that had zero confirmed atmospheres until now
audience: Technically curious engineers and researchers tracking astrobiology and exoplanet science milestones

title: AWS: Inaccurate Estimated Billing Data – $1.7 billion
why: If you run anything on AWS, the console's estimated-charges figure just proved unreliable enough to show a sub-$5 account a $1.7 billion bill, so any billing alerts or budget automation keyed to that number can fire on garbage.
notes: An AWS user reported an estimated bill of $1.7 billion for the current month against normal monthly spend under $5.
The report is a Hacker News post pointing to the AWS Health status page (health.aws.amazon.com/health/status), a Reddit r/aws thread, and an open AWS support ticket.
Scale is inferred from community activity, not an AWS statement: the HN post hit 1,064 points and 646 comments, and the Reddit thread corroborates other affected accounts.
Users characterize it as a billing estimation/display error rather than real charges, but the source text contains no AWS confirmation of cause, blast radius, or that actual invoices are unaffected.
No root cause, timeline, or resolution is provided in the article; everything beyond the single user's screenshot-level report is unverified at time of posting.
summary: A Hacker News thread surfaced reports that AWS's estimated billing display showed grossly inflated figures, including one account with normal spend under $5 that displayed an estimated $1.7 billion charge for the month. The affected user opened an urgent AWS support ticket and pointed to the AWS Health status page, with a Reddit r/aws thread suggesting other accounts saw similar anomalies. Community engagement was heavy (1,064 HN points, 646 comments), which is the basis for calling the issue widespread rather than an isolated account glitch. Users describe it as an estimation or display error, not actual charges levied, but the source provides no AWS confirmation of the cause, scope, or whether real invoices are affected. Root cause, timeline, and resolution are absent from the available text.
novelty: Active AWS platform incident: billing estimation system serving wildly inflated figures at scale, not an isolated account anomaly
audience: AWS users and cloud engineers who need to know this is a known platform-wide bug, not a security breach or actual charge

title: We're building Postgres in Rust. Using the LLVM of databases
why: If Turso pulls this off, Postgres-compatible SQL could run embedded in a browser tab or as a single file with no server process, changing how local-first and per-user database provisioning work for engineers.
notes: Architecture: Postgres SQL is parsed into a common AST and compiled to Turso's VDBE bytecode, the same bytecode layer the SQLite-compatible frontend already targets; Turso pitches this as the 'LLVM of databases' where one Rust core hosts many pluggable database frontends.

Status is explicitly a foundation, not a product: the experiment (originally 'pgmicro') is fully merged into the main tree, MIT-licensed, and buildable today via `cd postgres/cli && cargo run`, with no published packages yet.

Turso claims auto-updating (live) materialized views that need no REFRESH cron, and openly treats this as a deliberate divergence from Postgres semantics rather than 1:1 compatibility; they target a 'large enough subset to be useful in practice within a couple of months,' not 100% compatibility.
Proof that the VDBE is a real bytecode VM: they wrote a C-to-VDBE compiler and run Doom in-browser via WASM, with each rendered frame returned as a result row and memory regions represented as blobs.

Postgres extensions are handled via a WASM-container proof of concept with an acknowledged performance penalty; Turso warns not all extensions will load and argues arbitrary-code extension systems are a bad idea. PL/pgSQL would likely be replaced by 'something else' plus a compatibility layer.

Caveats attributed to the team: Postgres stores rows in heap files with separate index B-trees, TIDs, tuple versioning, and vacuum while SQLite clusters into the B-tree, differences they frame as on-disk layout rather than fundamental operations; plans include the Postgres wire protocol and a server implementation. Community is ~260+ contributors on the existing SQLite frontend.
summary: Turso, a from-scratch Rust rewrite of SQLite, is announcing a Postgres-compatible frontend built on the same core, positioning itself as the 'LLVM of databases': one modern reliable engine with multiple SQL frontends compiled down to SQLite's VDBE bytecode. Postgres SQL is parsed to a common AST and lowered to VDBE, the argument being that internally most SQL databases are collections of B-trees and indexes even if Postgres and SQLite differ in on-disk layout (heap files, TIDs, vacuum vs clustered B-trees). The work began as an experiment called pgmicro and is now merged into the main tree under MIT, buildable from source but with no released packages. Turso stresses this is an early foundation, not a finished product, and does not aim for 100% Postgres compatibility; it highlights divergences such as live auto-updating materialized views and plans a WASM-based approach to extensions and PL/pgSQL. To demonstrate the VDBE is a genuine bytecode VM, they compiled Doom from C to VDBE and run it in-browser via WASM, with each frame as a result row. The team frames the pitch around server-less, file-based, embeddable Postgres for local-first and agent-driven per-user database scenarios, leaning on its open-contribution community and a slow-and-correct development philosophy.
novelty: Postgres SQL compiled to SQLite's VDBE bytecode via a pluggable frontend on Turso's Rust core — "LLVM of databases" architecture with a running proof-of-concept already merged to main tree
audience: Systems engineers evaluating embedded/edge database stacks or Postgres-compatible alternatives

title: Lobste.rs is now running on SQLite
why: A rare, honestly told production war story showing exactly what breaks (and what it costs) when a real Rails/MariaDB app moves to SQLite, useful the next time someone pitches "just use SQLite."
notes: The final deploy landed July 11th (announced after surviving the following Monday traffic spike); reported results: lower CPU, lower memory, a snappier feel, and a projected ~50% VPS cost cut once the MariaDB instance is decommissioned.
The first deploy attempt (Feb 21st) failed even in read-only mode: read traffic alone pinned all CPUs at 100%, and the cause was only diagnosed after reverting.
Root cause was SQLite doing full table scans on the two largest tables for two queries plus an N+1 issue; three commits fixed all three problems before the successful retry.
K1 Investment Management's acquisition of MariaDB was the proximate catalyst that reopened the long-running (since issue #539 in 2019) Postgres-vs-SQLite migration debate in 2025.

No existing MariaDB/MySQL-to-SQLite conversion script was deemed adequate, so the author wrote a custom db-to-db migration script; generating a local test dataset at half of production size via a bulk-creation script took about a week.

The post is authored by a contributor working with @pushcx; both deploys used a pre-agreed deployment/revert checklist, and a slow query log was added before the second deploy as a safeguard. Note: the article's 'lessons' sections are left as empty headers, so no detailed takeaways are spelled out.
summary: Lobste.rs migrated its production stack from MariaDB to SQLite, closing a migration effort (issue #539) that had been debated since 2019 and was restarted in 2025 after K1's acquisition of MariaDB.
